questions.question: Implement a dynamic array class with push_back, pop_back, and get operations. The array should automatically resize when full.
keyPoints: Store data pointer, size, and capacity
Initialize with some initial capacity
Double capacity when size equals capacity
Copy elements during resize using realloc or manual copy
Handle edge cases (empty array, index out of bounds)

questions.question: Explain amortized analysis for dynamic array append operation. Prove that it is O(1) amortized.
keyPoints: Individual append: O(1) or O(n) when resize happens
Resize happens when size = capacity, doubles capacity
For n appends: copies are n/2 + n/4 + n/8 + ... < n
Total operations: n (appends) + n (copies) = 2n
Amortized cost per operation: 2n/n = 2 = O(1)

questions.question: Compare dynamic arrays with linked lists. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Dynamic Array: O(1) access, O(n) insert/delete in middle
Linked List: O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete with pointer
Dynamic Array: cache-friendly, contiguous memory
Linked List: no resize needed, but pointer overhead
Use dynamic array: frequent access, mostly end operations
Use linked list: frequent insert/delete in middle

questions.question: Why do dynamic arrays typically shrink when size falls below capacity/4, not capacity/2?
keyPoints: Prevents thrashing (repeated grow/shrink)
At capacity/2: one add → grow, one remove → shrink, repeat
At capacity/4: buffer zone before shrinking
Trade-off: slightly more memory waste vs better performance
Also known as hysteresis in shrinking

questions.question: Write a function to insert an element at a specific index in a dynamic array. Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Check if resize needed
Shift elements from index to end rightward
Insert element at given index
Time: O(n) due to shifting
Handle edge cases: invalid index, insertion at end

questions.question: Explain the concept of geometric growth in dynamic arrays and its advantages over additive growth.
keyPoints: Geometric growth: capacity doubles with each resize
Additive growth: capacity increases by a fixed amount
Geometric growth: O(1) amortized append, O(n) worst-case
Additive growth: O(n) amortized append, O(n) worst-case
Geometric growth is more efficient for frequent appends

questions.question: Compare the memory overhead of dynamic arrays with other data structures.
keyPoints: Dynamic Array: O(n) memory overhead due to capacity
Linked List: O(n) memory overhead due to pointers
Stack/Queue: O(n) memory overhead due to capacity
Trade-off: memory overhead vs performance

questions.question: Write a function to delete an element at a specific index in a dynamic array. Analyze its time complexity.
keyPoints: Check if index is valid
Shift elements from index to end leftward
Update size and capacity if necessary
Time: O(n) due to shifting
Handle edge cases: invalid index, deletion at end
